title: Untitled
research_problem: This study addresses the lack of pedestrian behavior models for signalized crosswalks, where pedestrian behavior involves group/individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and response to signal control and crosswalk boundary. The research aims to develop a microscopic simulation model for safety assessment at signalized intersections.
method_summary: A modified social force model was developed for pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks, incorporating five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians (both group and individual evasive behavior), repulsive force from conflicting left-turning vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. The model includes desired direction model using Weibull distribution and stop/go decision model for pedestrian flashing green phase. Parameters were calibrated by separating measurable parameters (directly estimated from observed trajectory data) from non-measurable parameters (derived by maximum likelihood estimation).
datasets: Kanayama intersection north crosswalk in Nagoya City, Japan - 1352 pedestrian trajectories
metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position estimation
Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed estimation

main_claims.claim_text: The modified social force model can accurately reproduce pedestrian trajectory with MAPE of 5.7% for position and 10.5% for speed
The model successfully represents lane-formation phenomenon with number of formed lanes (3.5) closer to observed (2.8) compared to non-route-choice model (4.1)
The model can represent pedestrian collision avoidance behavior with turning vehicles, with passing position shifting from 0 to 2-2.5m when conflicts exist
Separating parameters into measurable and non-measurable groups for calibration is an effective approach achieving favorable calibration results
The desired direction model using Weibull distribution with influencing factors (crosswalk geometry, OD movement, pedestrian density) reasonably reflects pedestrian behavior characteristics

limitations.text: The reaction of pedestrians to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world (peak shift 2.5m vs 2m observed)
Model calibrated using data from only one intersection (Kanayama North crosswalk), limiting generalizability
Pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics (age, gender, partner relationship) not considered as influencing factors
Only left-turning vehicles considered; right-turning and through vehicles not modeled

future_work: Improve pedestrian reaction sensitivity to vehicles to match real-world behavior
Incorporate pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics including age, gender, and partner relationships
Develop more elaborate modeling approach for diverse vehicle types and interactions
Conduct more strict calibration procedures with data from multiple intersections
Extend model to consider right-turning vehicles and other vehicle movements
notes: Published in Transportation Research Part C: Emerging Technologies, Volume 40, March 2014, Pages 143-159. The study is part of efforts to develop microscopic simulation for safety assessment at signalized intersections. Japan has over 30% of traffic accident fatalities being pedestrians at crosswalks. The model can visually reproduce pedestrian crossing behavior as in the real world.
